does: This paper presents ALMoST, an alignment framework that trains large language models using entirely synthetic feedback generated from contrasting responses of different LLM sizes and prompts, without requiring human annotations or proprietary models like ChatGPT. The method first performs reward modeling by comparing vanilla LLM outputs, then uses the learned reward model to generate high-quality synthetic demonstrations for supervised fine-tuning, and finally applies reinforcement learning for further optimization. The resulting model achieves superior performance on alignment benchmarks compared to open-source baselines trained on InstructGPT outputs or human annotations, with human evaluators preferring it over Alpaca 55% of the time and Dolly-v2 58.5% of the time.
connects: This work builds directly on the reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) paradigm established by InstructGPT and subsequent alignment literature, but addresses a core limitation: the dependency on expensive human annotations or proprietary model distillation. It extends reward modeling approaches by showing that contrastive signals between LLMs of different scales can serve as effective training signals without human intervention. The paper connects to distillation-free alignment methods and responds to the practical constraint that most researchers lack access to proprietary models or large-scale annotation budgets, synthesizing insights from multi-scale model comparison with standard fine-tuning and RL optimization pipelines.
enables: This approach opens pathways toward fully autonomous alignment of open-source models without human oversight or commercial dependencies, potentially democratizing access to well-aligned LLMs. The synthetic feedback mechanism could scale to continuously improving models as new base LLMs emerge, enabling rapid iteration on alignment methods. Future work could explore whether synthetic feedback quality improves through iterative model enhancement, how this scales to even larger models, and whether the method generalizes beyond instruction-following to other alignment objectives like fairness or robustness. The framework also suggests possibilities for domain-specific alignment where synthetic task-specific feedback could be generated without specialized human expertise.
